segments.s231-g9: This clause reports speech. بدو is the dative/enclitic 'to him' (lit. 'to + him'), and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'. رو is an imperative/command 'go' or 'turn (toward)' addressed to the addressee; پیش introduces the location 'before/in the presence of', and آن شیر مرد is a deictic + epithet 'that lion-man' (a brave/heroic man). Word order is somewhat inverted because this is poetic narration of an instruction.
segments.s231-g10: بگویش is an imperative 'tell/say to him' (بگو + -یش, the -ش being the older object pronoun 'to him'); که introduces the content of the command ('that' / 'that...'), تندی is the noun 'haste' or 'rashness', and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'. در نبرد is a locative prepositional phrase 'in battle' modifying the prohibition. Together they form the direct command quoted by the speaker.
segments.s231-g11: A poetic list of names/peoples joined by the copulative و 'and'. Each item is a proper-name or ethnonym; the final وهر is another name. In poetry such enumerations stand alone before the comment that follows.
segments.s231-g12: کزین (ک + ز + این) is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of this'; کینه is the noun 'hatred, grudge' as the object; هرگز is an adverb 'never'; ندارند is the present plural 'they do not have / they bear not' (نداشتن with present ending -ند); بهر is a directional/prepositional particle 'toward/for' (here marking the target/direction of the feeling). The clause means they never hold any grudge on this account.
segments.s231-g13: A paired identification: یکی 'one/a certain' introduces each clause, followed by the title شاه 'king' plus the place-name (ختلان, چین). This inverted, terser word order (article/quantifier + predicate + name) is typical in epic listing: 'one (is) the king of Khotlan, one (is) the king of China.'
segments.s231-g14: ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic variant), بیگانه مردم 'foreign peoples' is the noun phrase (adjective + noun). ترا is the dative/accusative 'to you' realized as ت + را (second-person suffix plus object marker), نیست is the copula/negator 'there is not / do not', and کین is the noun 'enmity, grudge'. Grammatically this says 'from foreign peoples there is no grudge toward you' (i.e. foreigners bear you no enmity).
segments.s231-g15: Again an identification: یکی 'one/a certain' + شهریارست, here functioning as a predicate 'is king/ruler' (شهریار + -ست 'is'); افراسیاب is the proper name in apposition. The verb/copula is embedded in شهریارست, so the name follows the predicate — a typical poetic inversion ('one is king, Afrasiab').
segments.s231-g16: که is the relative/subordinator 'who/that'; آتش 'fire' is the subject of the following verb. همی is the archaic continuous/iterative particle (marks habitual/present action); بد is an adverb/adjective 'bad/ill' modifying the verb; شناسد is the present-tense verb 'knows/recognizes' (here 'regards' or 'judge[s]'); ز آب 'from/from water' (i.e. 'in comparison with water' or 'as against water'). Together the relative clause says 'who regards fire badly compared with water' (cf. 'who thinks fire worse than water').
segments.s231-g25: نداری here is a nominal/abstract predicate meaning 'lack' or 'you have no …' with an emphatic همانا 'truly / verily' immediately after it for emphasis. ز is the poetic short form of از 'from / because of', governing the following noun phrase خاقان چین 'khaghan's wrinkle' (چین = 'wrinkle' here), so the whole segment says there is no sign/wrinkle caused by the khaghan.
segments.s231-g26: ز کار گذشته is a prepositional phrase 'from / on account of past deed(s)' (گذشته = 'past'). بدل is used in the sense of 'retaliation / in return' and is followed by هیچ کین 'no/none of any grudge/vengeance' — word order is poetic (بدل هیـچ کین = 'no retaliation/vengeance in return'), expressing that no vengeance will be exacted for past actions.
segments.s231-g27: چنو (classical/poetic variant of چنان/چون) functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/if'. باز گردد is a third‑person present/subjunctive/optative form of گردیدن 'to return/turn', here 'he returns' (subject omitted and understood from context). تو زو باز گرد is an imperative/address to 'you' (تو) with زو = از او 'from him' and باز گرد 'turn back / withdraw' (verb in the imperative placed after the object for emphasis).
segments.s231-g28: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for / because'. اکنون 'now'; سپه را uses را as the definite object/ topical marker with سپه 'army, host'; سرآمد is used as a noun 'conclusion / end' and نبرد 'battle' names the struggle — together 'the army's battle has reached its conclusion', giving the reason for withdrawing.
segments.s231-g29: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. کاموس here is a proper name (not one of the listed dictionary senses), so it refers to the person Kamus. بر دست تو literally 'by your hand' (بر + دست used to mark agent/instrument), کشته is the past participle 'killed', and شد is the auxiliary 'became/was' forming the past passive 'was slain'.
segments.s231-g30: سر here means 'chief / leader' (the heads of the warriors). رزمجویان 'warriors' is the genitive/possessed group; همه 'all' quantifies them. گشته شد is a compound where گشته (perfect participle 'turned / become') plus auxiliary شد yields 'were turned / became' — in context indicating that the leaders among the warriors were all turned (i.e., put to flight or changed state).
segments.s231-g31: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'he gave such an answer' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave' as the verb, پاسخ 'reply'). که here introduces the content of that reply ('that ...'). پیلان 'elephants' (plural) and تاج 'crown' are the items named in the reported speech, coordinated by و.
segments.s231-g32: بنزدیک من باید is a small clause meaning 'should be / ought to be near me' where بنزدیک 'near' precedes the possessor من 'me', and باید (modal) indicates obligation/entitlement ('must / should belong'). و links to تخت عاج 'an ivory throne' (تخت 'throne' + عاج 'ivory'), the final item of his demand/claim.
segments.s231-g1: نگه کرد is a past action 'fixed his gaze / looked' (verb + کرد). In this poetic order the verb precedes the subject خاقان; ازان پشت پیل is a prepositional phrase 'from that back of the elephant' (ازان = 'from that', پشت = 'back', پیل = 'elephant') with the usual omission of an explicit ezafe in verse.
segments.s231-g2: زمین دید is 'he saw the earth/land' (دید past of 'to see'). برسان here functions idiomatically as 'reaching to / stretching as far as' (literally 'bring to'), followed by دریای نیل 'the Sea of Nile' (دریای = 'sea of', نیل = 'Nile'). The clause describes the extent of the view.
segments.s231-g3: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain'; نامداری here introduces a notable person/thing ('a famed one')—poetic usage where a noun standing after یکی specifies the 'one'. بر + پشت + کوه بلند = 'upon the back of the high mountain' (preposition + noun phrase). بجست is past 'leapt / sprang' used idiomatically as 'stepped forward / stood' in narrative.
segments.s231-g4: ورا = 'to it / upon it' (dative/locative); نام = 'name'; بد is an adjective 'bad / ominous' qualifying نام (i.e. 'an ill-omened / fearsome name'); رستم دیو بند is a compound/name in apposition ('Rostam, the demon‑binder'). The phrase as a whole states that such a name was applied to that elephant/thing.
segments.s231-g5: همی is an adverb of continuity 'continually / kept on'; کرگس is the subject 'vulture'; آورد (past) 'brought / came' with ز + ابر سیاه = 'from / with a black cloud' (ز = 'from', ابر = 'cloud', سیاه = 'black/dark'). The clause describes an ongoing action.
segments.s231-g6: نظاره is a noun 'gaze / sight' used with بران ('upon that') to mark the target of the gaze; اختر و چرخ ماه lists the targets 'that star and the wheel/sky of the moon' (چرخ here = 'heaven / celestial sphere', ماه = 'moon'). Grammatically the phrase says the vulture directed its gaze upon those celestial objects.
segments.s231-g7: یکی = 'a certain'; نامداری = 'a renowned/notable one' (used as a substantive); ز لشکر = 'from the army/host' (ز = 'from'); بجست is past 'leapt / sprang' used idiomatically 'stepped forward'. The whole segment introduces a famous man emerging from the ranks.
segments.s231-g8: که introduces a relative clause 'who'; گفتار ایران = 'the speech/words of Iran' (i.e. counsel or what is proper for Iran); بداند is the verb 'to know' (here 'knew' / 'was able to know/express'); درست modifies the verb as 'correctly / rightly'. Together: 'who knew how to speak/express rightly for Iran.'
segments.s231-g33: بتاراج ایران is a genitive noun phrase 'the plundering of Iran' (بتاراج governs ایران). نهادست is an archaic/copular verb meaning 'is placed' or 'is inherent' (3rd‑person present), so the clause says that this plundering is a settled fact or disposition. روی follows as a postpositional/locative element 'upon' (classical word order places such particles after the copula or phrase).
segments.s231-g34: چه begins a rhetorical question 'what' / 'what use'. باید is an impersonal/modal 'is necessary' / 'is there need' and combines with کنون 'now' (adverb) to mean 'is there need now'. لابه و گفت و گوی is a coordinated noun phrase 'entreaty/pleading and speech/talk' (connected by و), with گفت and گوی functioning as nouns here rather than verbs.
segments.s231-g35: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. داند is 3rd‑person present 'he knows'. که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. لشکر بجنگ آمدست is the subordinate clause: بجنگ is an infinitival/locative phrase 'to battle/for fighting' and آمدست (an older perfective/copular) means 'has come', so together 'the army has come to fight.'
segments.s231-g36: This clause names the source/cause: شتاب سپاه is a construct 'the haste of the host' (شتاب as noun + سپاه as its possessor). از marks origin/cause 'from/because of', درنگ is 'delay/pausing', and آمدست ('has come') is the verb stating that the haste 'arose/has come' from that delay.
segments.s231-g37: فرستاده گفت is the reporting clause 'the messenger said'. The direct speech begins with the vocative ای 'O', followed by خداوند 'lord' used as an honorific; رخش follows as a genitive/appositive identifying which lord is meant ('lord of Rakhsh' — i.e., Rostam). Note that گفت here functions as the past reporting verb 'said'.
segments.s231-g38: بدشت is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to the plain' or 'into the plain'. آهوی ناگرفته is a noun phrase 'the gazelle not taken' where ناگرفته is a participial adjective 'not taken / not yet captured'. مبخش is a negative imperative 'do not give / do not grant', placed at the end as the main verb of the command.
segments.s231-g39: The first که heads an indirect/questionive clause; داند is 'knows' (3rd person). The second که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. خود 'himself' is reflexive/emphatic; چون here means 'how' (introducing the manner/condition); بود is the copula 'was/was like' (classical past), and روزگار means 'fortune/lot' — together rendering 'who knows how his own fortune fares.'
segments.s231-g40: که again introduces the subordinate clause of possibility/result. پیروز is an adjective 'victorious'. برگردد is a 3rd‑person verbal form 'may return / will return' (classical present/subjunctive nuance of potential return), and از کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'from the battlefield'. The clause asks whether he might come back victorious from battle.
segments.s231-g17: بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning “in this way” (بدین = به/ب + this, گونه = manner). The verb کرد (‘made/called’) follows the adverbial, and انجمن is the object meaning “assembly/council” (i.e. ‘he called an assembly thus’). Word order is Topic–Adverbial–Verb–Object here, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s231-g18: بد آورد is a verbal phrase meaning ‘brought ill’ or ‘caused misfortune’ (بد as adjective/adverb + آورد ‘brought/caused’). ازین رزم is a prepositional phrase ‘by/from this battle’ (ازین = از + این), and بر خویشتن ‘upon himself’ uses بر with the emphatic reflexive خویشتن: the whole clause says he caused harm onto himself through the battle.
segments.s231-g19: کسی نیست is the existential negative ‘there is no one’. A string of adjectival phrases follows: بی‌آز, بی‌نام and (poetically ellided بی‑) ننگ — the negative prefix بی‑ applies to the listed qualities, with the last بی omitted by ellipsis (a common poetic economy). The adjectives describe absence of trial/renown/shame.
segments.s231-g20: همان here is a discourse adverb meaning ‘that very / indeed’. آشتی بهتر آید is a fixed evaluative clause ‘reconciliation/peace comes better’ = ‘is preferable’. ز جنگ uses poetic ز for the comparative ‘than/from war’. The verb آید is present/simple-predicative (‘comes/is’).
segments.s231-g21: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause ‘a messenger came’. بر پیلتن uses بر in the directional sense ‘to/toward Pilten’ (a place-name). Subject–verb–direction order is straightforward here.
segments.s231-g22: زبان پر ز گفتار is a verbless descriptive phrase: ‘tongue full of speech’ where پر ‘full (of)’ follows the noun and is completed by the genitive-like ز گفتار. Likewise دل پر شکن = ‘a heart full of شکن’ (شکن = tormentor/malice). The pattern Noun + پر + ز + Noun is a common phrase-building device meaning ‘N full of N2’.
segments.s231-g23: بدو گفت literally ‘he said to him’ (بدو = به + او, ‘to him’). کای is the vocative particle ‘O’, addressing someone; مهتر رزمجوی is the vocative title ‘chief / senior warrior’ — nouns in vocative follow کای and are directly addressed.
segments.s231-g24: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when’. رزمت is رزم + ‑ت (the suffix ‑ت = your), ‘your battle/fighting’. سرآمد is ‘end/conclusion’ (here ‘is ended’), کنون ‘now’. بزم جوی is an exhortation: بزم (‘feast/assembly’) + جوی (from جستن, here imperative/command sense ‘seek!’ or ‘turn to’). So the clause reads ‘When your fighting has ended, now seek the feast.’
segments.s231-g41: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. بشنید is simple past 'he heard' (third-person singular). The subject رستم follows the verb here (poetic inversion) and برانگیخت is a past transitive 'roused/spurred'; رخش is the direct object 'Rakhsh' (the horse). The clause therefore reads 'When Rostam heard, he roused Rakhsh.'
segments.s231-g42: منم is an emphatic self-identification 'it is I' (من + enclitic -م). گفت here functions as the speech-verb 'said' introducing reported speech (the provided lexical senses list did not include this past-tense meaning, so see note). شیراوژن تاج‌بخش is the speaker's name plus epithet in apposition after the verb: 'Shirāvazhan, the crown-giver.' Word order (verb before subject/epithet) is a poetic inversion.
segments.s231-g43: تنی زورمند is a descriptive nominal phrase 'strong-bodied' (تنی = 'body/physical', زورمند = 'powerful'). The prefixed preposition-like بـ attached in ببازو (ب + بازو) means 'by/with the arm.' کمند is 'lasso/snare.' The whole phrase lists attributes/arms: 'strong-bodied, and with arm and lasso.'
segments.s231-g44: چه is exclamatory 'what/how' introducing an evaluative clause. روز here means 'time/era' rather than the literal 'day.' فریبست is the fused predicate 'فریب است' = 'it is deception/treacherous'; this is an archaic/poetic copular form. و هنگام بند is coordinated: 'and (it is) the time of bonds/fettering' (هنگام = 'the time of', بند = 'fetter, bond').
segments.s231-g45: چه again is the exclamatory 'what' (rhetorical). خاقان چینی is 'the Chinese khaghan' (noun + adjective following, common in Persian). کمند مرا literally places 'snare' plus 'مرا' ('to me' / 'me') with the copular idea left implicit; poetic ellipsis implies 'is to me' (i.e. 'what is the Chinese khaghan's snare to me?'). The separate token م is the reduced/copular element (lemma noted as #هست) used in older/poetic forms, and را appears as the object/topic marker.
segments.s231-g46: Parallel to the previous clause: چه introduces a rhetorical question. شیر = 'lion' and ژیان here points to 'life/livelihood' or a lion-like quality (together functioning as a lion-epithet). دست‌بند (dast-band) is the compound 'hand-fetter/bracelet/handcuff' (دست + بند). Again م (copula) and را (object/topic marker) complete the elliptical rhetorical question: 'what is a lion's hand-fetter to me?'

sentences.s231-s5: He said to him, "Go up before that lion of a man and tell him: ‘Do not be rash in battle.’"
sentences.s231-s6: The Chaghani and Shagani and the Chinese and Vahr — from this they never bear any hatred.
sentences.s231-s7: One is the king of Khotlan, another the king of China; foreign peoples harbor no grudge against you.
sentences.s231-s8: One is the ruler Afrasiab, who regards fire ill in comparison with water.
sentences.s231-s13: You have, truly, no wrinkle because of the khaghan; from past deeds there is no retaliation or grudge.
sentences.s231-s14: When he returns, you too withdraw from him; for now the army's fighting has reached its end.
sentences.s231-s15: When Kamus was slain by your hand, the chiefs of the warriors were all turned (i.e., were put to flight / changed).
sentences.s231-s16: He gave this answer: 'Elephants and the crown should be near me, and an ivory throne.'
sentences.s231-s1: The khagan looked from that elephant's back and saw the earth stretching to the Nile Sea.
sentences.s231-s2: One elephant stood upon the high back of a mountain; to it they gave the ominous name 'Rostam, the demon‑binder'.
sentences.s231-s3: A vulture came with a black cloud, continually fixing its gaze on that star and the moon's heavens.
sentences.s231-s4: A renowned man from the host sprang forward, one who knew how to speak rightly for Iran.
sentences.s231-s17: The plundering of Iran is inborn upon him; what use now are entreaties and talk?
sentences.s231-s18: When he knows that the host has come to fight, the army's hurry sprang from the delay.
sentences.s231-s19: The messenger said, 'O lord of Rakhsh, do not give the gazelle not yet taken into the plain.'
sentences.s231-s20: Who can tell what his own fortunes are — whether he will return victorious from the battlefield?
sentences.s231-s9: He called together the world's assembly in this way; by this battle he brought ill upon himself.
sentences.s231-s10: There is no one untried, nameless, or without shame; indeed reconciliation is preferable to war.
sentences.s231-s11: A messenger came to Pilten, tongue full of speech and a heart full of malice.
sentences.s231-s12: He said to him, 'O chief war‑leader, now that your fighting is over, seek the feast.'
sentences.s231-s21: When Rostam heard (this), he spurred Rakhsh; "It is I," said Shirāvazhan, the crown-giver.
sentences.s231-s22: Strong of body and with arm and lasso; what a deceitful day and a time for bonds.
sentences.s231-s23: What is the Chinese khaghan's snare to me? What is the lion's hand-fetter to me?
